---
title: "Dictionaries"
description: "Use key-value mappings for lookup, looping, counting, grouping, and simple records."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Why this matters

Use key-value mappings for lookup, looping, counting, grouping, and simple records.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one mental model.

::: {.questions}
### Questions

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Dictionaries** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning objectives

You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 3 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- Section 1: **Dictionaries** — Store key-value pairs for fast lookup by meaningful labels.
- Section 2: **Dictionary Looping** — Loop through dictionary keys, values, and key-value pairs.
- Section 3: **Dictionary Patterns** — Use dictionaries for counting, grouping, and simple records.

<!-- mermaid-visual-model -->
## Visual model

::: {.visual-model}
A dictionary is a lookup table. You provide a key; Python returns the value stored
under that key.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  key1["key<br/>'name'"] --> value1["value<br/>'Ada'"]
  key2["key<br/>'role'"] --> value2["value<br/>'programmer'"]
  key3["key<br/>'language'"] --> value3["value<br/>'Python'"]
  lookup["person['name']"] --> key1
  value1 --> result["result<br/>'Ada'"]
```

When a `KeyError` appears, compare the key you asked for with the keys actually
inside the dictionary.
:::

## Section 1: Dictionaries

Store key-value pairs for fast lookup by meaningful labels.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A dictionary is like a real dictionary: look up a word and get its definition.
:::

### Mental model

A dictionary maps unique keys to values.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
person = {"name": "Ada", "role": "programmer"}
print(person["name"])
person["language"] = "Python"
print(person)
```

### Walkthrough

1. `person = {"name": "Ada", "role": "programmer"}` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `print(person["name"])` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `person["language"] = "Python"` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(person)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Looking up a missing key raises KeyError. Use in or .get() when a key may be absent.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-structures-dictionaries",
  "title": "Dictionaries checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Dictionaries?",
      "options": [
        "A dictionary maps unique keys to values.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A dictionary maps unique keys to values."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A dictionary is like a real dictionary: look up a word and get its definition."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 2: Dictionary Looping

Loop through dictionary keys, values, and key-value pairs.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Looping through a dictionary is like reading a contact list: you can read names, phone
numbers, or both together.
:::

### Mental model

Dictionary loops let you inspect labels and values in a mapping.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
scores = {"Ada": 95, "Grace": 98}
for name, score in scores.items():
    print(f"{name}: {score}")
```

### Walkthrough

1. `scores = {"Ada": 95, "Grace": 98}` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `for name, score in scores.items():` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `print(f"{name}: {score}")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Looping over a dictionary directly gives keys, not values. Use .items() when you need
both.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-structures-dictionary-looping",
  "title": "Dictionary Looping checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Dictionary Looping?",
      "options": [
        "Dictionary loops let you inspect labels and values in a mapping.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Dictionary loops let you inspect labels and values in a mapping."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Looping through a dictionary is like reading a contact list: you can read names, phone numbers, or both together."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 3: Dictionary Patterns

Use dictionaries for counting, grouping, and simple records.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A counting dictionary is a scoreboard: each key has a score that changes as events
happen.
:::

### Mental model

Dictionaries shine when you need to update a value associated with a label.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
counts = {}
for letter in "banana":
    counts[letter] = counts.get(letter, 0) + 1
print(counts)
```

### Walkthrough

1. `counts = {}` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `for letter in "banana":` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `counts[letter] = counts.get(letter, 0) + 1` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(counts)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Starting from an empty dictionary requires a default value before adding 1. .get(key, 0)
is a common pattern.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-structures-dictionary-patterns",
  "title": "Dictionary Patterns checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Dictionary Patterns?",
      "options": [
        "Dictionaries shine when you need to update a value associated with a label.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Dictionaries shine when you need to update a value associated with a label."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A counting dictionary is a scoreboard: each key has a score that changes as events happen."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
tiny example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- **Dictionaries**: A dictionary maps unique keys to values.
- **Dictionary Looping**: Dictionary loops let you inspect labels and values in a mapping.
- **Dictionary Patterns**: Dictionaries shine when you need to update a value associated with a label.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Python data structures tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html
- Built-in types: https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
